skill.name: azure-mgmt-fabric-dotnet
skill.description: Guides .NET developers through Azure Resource Manager operations for Microsoft Fabric capacities, including provisioning, scaling, suspension, resumption, deletion, SKU discovery, and name availability checks.
skill.summary: Manage Microsoft Fabric capacities with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command execution and network warnings are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, inline .NET type names, and reference links. No prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or executable install script was found. The skill carries medium operational risk because it guides authenticated Azure management operations, including creating, scaling, suspending, resuming, and deleting Fabric capacities.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Azure Resource Mutation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill shows management-plane operations that can create, scale, suspend, resume, and delete Microsoft Fabric capacities. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but agents should require explicit user approval before running these operations against a real subscription.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly invoke Azure Resource Manager operations that modify or delete cloud resources. The behavior is expected for this skill, but the operational impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Azure Credential Environment Variables Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill documents service principal environment variables, including a client secret placeholder. This is common Azure guidance and no exfiltration is present, but users should avoid exposing secrets to logs or prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names are explicit and include AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET. The surrounding content uses placeholders and DefaultAzureCredential, so the risk is handling exposure rather than malicious collection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are in Markdown code fences and inline code references, not executable Ruby or shell automation shipped by the skill. The only real shell commands shown install NuGet packages for the documented SDK workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the suspicious backticks are formatting delimiters. No executable script file or automatic command runner is present.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are documentation and package reference links. They do not send data, call webhooks, or instruct credential transmission to a third party.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to NuGet, GitHub, and Microsoft Learn references. There is no code that performs network requests with user or environment data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding at the front matter description has no matching cryptographic code or instruction. No evidence found for hashing, encryption, or cryptographic downgrade behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 begins a YAML description field for Azure Fabric management. The reviewed content contains no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts are triggered by ordinary Azure state and validation wording. They do not enumerate the host system, scan networks, or probe external targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe provisioning states, exception messages, and administrator validity. They are not commands or logic that inspect local systems or networks.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The scanner combined command examples, reference links, and Azure credential placeholders into a critical heuristic. Context review found documentation only, with no automatic execution path or data exfiltration pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ingredients for the heuristic are present as documentation examples, not as integrated executable behavior. Medium operational risk remains because the examples manage real Azure resources.

dangerous_patterns.title: Long-Running Cloud Provisioning Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use completed Azure Resource Manager operations for capacity creation and update. These operations can incur cost or change production capacity when run against a real subscription.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples call CreateOrUpdateAsync and UpdateAsync against Fabric capacity resources. These are normal ARM SDK calls but have direct cloud-side effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Capacity Deletion Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes a delete operation for Fabric capacity resources. This is legitimate documentation, but it should require explicit confirmation before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show DeleteAsync on a capacity resource. The destructive operation is explicit and semantically clear.

content.value_statement: Microsoft Fabric capacity management through Azure Resource Manager can be complex and cost-sensitive. This skill provides focused .NET SDK guidance for provisioning, scaling, pausing, deleting, and inspecting Fabric capacities.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Azure.ResourceManager.Fabric and Azure.Identity packages.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential setup for Fabric capacity management.
Provides .NET examples for creating and reading Fabric capacities.
Covers scaling, administrator updates, suspension, resumption, and deletion.
Lists SDK types used for Fabric capacity resource workflows.
Documents name availability checks, SKU discovery, and common ARM errors.
limitations: Covers management-plane Fabric capacity resources only.
Does not manage Fabric workspaces, lakehouses, warehouses, or data items.
Requires valid Azure permissions, quota, and subscription context.
Examples may need adjustment for local naming, region, and governance policies.

use_cases.title: Provision Fabric Capacity
use_cases.description: Create a Fabric capacity with a selected SKU, location, administrators, and tags through the .NET ARM SDK.

use_cases.title: Control Capacity Costs
use_cases.description: Suspend idle Fabric capacities, resume them when needed, and choose smaller development SKUs before production scale-up.

use_cases.title: Build Capacity Automation
use_cases.description: Add name checks, SKU listing, error handling, and state checks to repeatable provisioning workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Capacity
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Fabric capacity.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the azure-mgmt-fabric-dotnet skill, show me the .NET steps to create a Microsoft Fabric capacity in an existing resource group.

prompt_templates.title: Check Names and SKUs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how I can check Fabric capacity name availability and list supported SKUs before provisioning.

prompt_templates.title: Scale and Pause Capacity
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through updating a Fabric capacity SKU, changing administrators, suspending the capacity, and resuming it with the .NET SDK.

prompt_templates.title: Design Automation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to design a robust Fabric capacity automation flow with DefaultAzureCredential, idempotent create-or-update logic, state checks, and ARM error handling.

output_examples.input: Create a Fabric capacity in West US 2 with F64.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step .NET workflow that authenticates, loads the resource group, defines administrators and SKU, starts the create operation, and reports the resulting state.

output_examples.input: Find the right SKU before scaling.
output_examples.output: A concise plan for listing subscription SKUs, checking capacity-specific scale options, comparing regions, and selecting a valid target SKU.
Guidance for catching ARM request failures, identifying conflicts, invalid configuration, permission issues, quota problems, and retry decisions.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and avoid placing secrets in prompts, source files, or logs.
Confirm subscription, resource group, region, SKU, and billing impact before running mutating operations.
Check provisioning and resource state before scaling, suspending, resuming, or deleting a capacity.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for Fabric data-plane workspaces, lakehouses, warehouses, or item APIs.
Do not run delete, scale, suspend, or resume examples without explicit production approval.
Do not assume every Fabric SKU is available in every Azure region or subscription.

faq.question: What does this skill manage?
faq.answer: It manages Microsoft Fabric capacity resources through Azure Resource Manager using the .NET SDK.

faq.question: Does it manage Fabric workspaces or lakehouses?
faq.answer: No. It is limited to management-plane capacity operations. Use Fabric data-plane APIs for workspace and item operations.

faq.question: Which authentication approach does it show?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential from Azure.Identity and documents common Azure environment variables.

faq.question: Can it help reduce Fabric capacity costs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes suspension and resumption guidance, and recommends smaller SKUs for development and testing.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. It is documentation, but it describes Azure operations that can change resources and billing.

faq.question: What should I confirm before using the examples?
faq.answer: Confirm the target subscription, resource group, region, SKU, permissions, quota, administrators, and approval for any mutating operation.
